summary: WebAssembly (WASM) could enable the decentralized data sharing vision of Data Mesh by allowing databases to run directly in browsers, bypassing centralized cloud infrastructure. This mirrors the peer-to-peer ethos of the early internet, where information flowed freely before large monopolies took control.
key_points: WASM enables running databases inside web browsers, opening new possibilities for data applications
Modern personal computers have vastly more power than most browser-based tasks require
Data Mesh's core vision is about decentralized data sharing and individual control over data
Projects like Motherduck, SingleStore+WASM, and SQLite+WASM are pioneering browser-based databases
True decentralization requires avoiding any single monolithic product or vendor
money_quotes: When I speak with Zhamak about her vision for Data Mesh, it comes from her dystopian fear that big companies and governments control access to data. In the end, data needs to be free.
Fast forward to today, and the Internet is mostly a cesspool controlled by large monopolies.
The equivalent of Napster is now possible for data products.
Data Mesh is also about avoiding any single monolithic product or vendor, as this defeats the purpose of decentralization.
key_insight: WASM's ability to run databases in browsers could restore the peer-to-peer ethos of the early internet to data sharing, enabling truly decentralized data products without dependence on centralized cloud providers.
